1:
שִׁגָּיוֹן (shiggāyôn) — a transliterated genre term appearing only here (the plural shigionoth in Hab 3:1). The derivation is uncertain: possibly from shāgāh (to wander, err, reel) suggesting an irregular, impassioned lyric; or from a cognate Akkadian root for lamentation. The LXX renders it "Psalm." Whatever the genre, it signals a distinctive, passionate form of expression.

The superscription references "Cush the Benjaminite" — an otherwise unknown figure who apparently slandered David; this personal historical hook gives the corporate lament a specific occasion.
2: פֶּן-יִטְרֹף כְּאַרְיֵה נַפְשִׁי (pen-yiṭrōp kĕʾaryēh napshî, "lest they tear my soul apart like a lion") — ṭārap: to tear, to rend (as a lion tears its prey); the verb is used of predatory animals. Nefesh (soul/life/person) as the target — the total person at risk of being torn apart. וְאֵין מַצִּיל (wĕʾên maṣṣîl, "with no one to rescue") — the hiphil participle of nāṣal: one who snatches away to safety. The situation is complete vulnerability with no human helper.
3: יְהוָה אֱלֹהַי אִם-עָשִׂיתִי זֹאת (YHWH ʾĕlōhay ʾim-ʿāśîtî zōʾt, "O LORD my God, if I have done this") — the conditional oath structure (v3-5) is a form of self-imprecation: "if I am guilty, then let the curse fall on me." The Psalmist invites divine judgment as proof of innocence — a legal procedure akin to a trial by ordeal, but directed to God rather than a human tribunal.
4: אִם-גָּמַלְתִּי שׁוֹלְמִי רָע (ʾim-gāmaltî shôlmî rāʿ, "if I have repaid evil to one who was at peace with me") — gāmal: to deal out, to repay; the word also means to wean (as weaning is completing a stage of nurturing). Shôlmî: the one who is at peace/shalom with me, one with whom I have a covenant of friendship. The specific denial is of betrayal of a peaceful relationship — directly relevant if the accusation involves treachery.
5: יִרְדֹּף אוֹיֵב נַפְשִׁי (yirdōp ʾôyēb napshî, "let the enemy pursue and overtake me") — the self-imprecation is fully stated: if guilty, let the worst happen. יִרְמֹס לָאָרֶץ חַיָּתִי (yirmōs lāʾāreṣ ḥayyātî, "trample my life into the ground") — rāmas: to trample down; ḥayyātî (my life/living self). כְּבוֹדִי לֶעָפָר יַשְׁכֵּן (kĕbôdî leʿāpār yashkên, "lay my honor in the dust") — kābôd (glory/honor/weight) reduced to ʿāpār (dust/dirt): the reversal of honor to shame. Selāh marks the weight of the oath.
6: קוּמָה יְהוָה בְּאַפֶּךָ (qûmāh YHWH bĕʾappekā, "Rise up, O LORD, in your anger") — the same call as Ps 3:7 but now invoked in the context of established innocence (vv3-5): because David is innocent, the enemy's rage is rebellion against the LORD's order. עוּרָה אֵלַי (ʿûrāh ʾêlay, "rouse yourself") — ʿûr: to awake from sleep, to bestir oneself (used of the divine "sleeping" in Ps 44:23). מִשְׁפָּט צִוִּיתָ (mishpāṭ ṣiwwîtāh, "you have decreed judgment") — the judgment is already established by divine command; God is called to execute what he has already decreed.
7: וַעֲדַת לְאֻמִּים (waʿădat lĕʾummîm, "the assembly of the peoples") — the image expands from David's personal enemies to a cosmic assize: all peoples gathered around the enthroned judge. עֲלֶיהָ לַמָּרוֹם שׁוּבָה (ʿălêhā lammārôm shûbāh, "take your seat on high above them") — God is called to ascend to the high seat of judgment above the assembly, not a retreat from the scene but an elevation to the judge's bench. The imagery is of a divine court session.
8: יְהוָה יָדִין עַמִּים (YHWH yādîn ʿammîm, "The LORD judges the peoples") — the imperfect here is a declaration of God's characteristic activity: this is what the LORD does, continually. שָׁפְטֵנִי יְהוָה כְּצִדְקִי (shāpĕṭênî YHWH kĕṣidqî, "vindicate me, O LORD, according to my righteousness") — the appeal to ṣedeq (righteousness) in the forensic sense: judge my case on the merits of my actual conduct, not the accusations of enemies. Ûtĕmimmî (my integrity/wholeness) — tōm: completeness, uprightness without a crack or hidden defect.
9: בֹּחֵן לִבּוֹת וּכְלָיוֹת (bōḥên libbôt ûkĕlāyôt, "the righteous God searches hearts and minds") — bāḥan: to test, assay (as metal is tested); the participle expresses characteristic ongoing action. Libbôt (hearts — plural of lēb) = the organ of thought, will, intention; kĕlāyôt (kidneys) = the deepest seat of emotion and secret purpose. Together they indicate the total interior life — nothing hidden from this divine search. Rev 2:23 applies this directly to Christ: "I am he who searches minds and hearts."
10: מָגִנִּי עַל-אֱלֹהִים (māginnî ʿal-ʾĕlōhîm, "My shield is God, the Most High") — māgên (small handheld shield, as in Ps 3:3) with the proposition ʿal (upon/above/through): the shield that is upon me comes from God. מוֹשִׁיעַ יִשְׁרֵי-לֵב (môshîaʿ yishĕrê-lēb, "who saves those who are upright in heart") — yāshar: straight, right; those whose heart is without crookedness. The divine savior (môshîaʿ) is the one who rescues the innocent, consistent with the appeal of vv3-8.
11: אֵל שׁוֹפֵט צַדִּיק (ʾêl shôpêṭ ṣaddîq, "God is a righteous judge") — three predicates: ʾêl (God/El), shôpêṭ (participle: continuously judging), ṣaddîq (righteous). The participle is important: not a future judgment only but a present, ongoing activity. וְאֵל זֹעֵם בְּכָל-יוֹם (wĕʾêl zōʿêm bĕkol-yôm, "a God who expresses indignation every day") — zāʿam: to be indignant, to be enraged; the participle again indicates habitual, ongoing activity. Divine anger is not periodic but continuous in response to ongoing wickedness — not stored up for an eventual reckoning but always present.
12: אִם-לֹא יָשׁוּב חַרְבּוֹ יִלְטוֹשׁ (ʾim-lōʾ yāshûb ḥarbô yilṭôsh, "If a man does not repent, God will sharpen his sword") — the "if" is conditional on continued unrepentance: shûb (to turn/repent) is the condition. Lāṭash: to sharpen, to whet (as a sword is sharpened on a stone). God is depicted as an armed judge preparing weapons — the imagery of divine warrior preparing for battle against the unrepentant. קַשְׁתּוֹ דָּרַךְ וַיְכוֹנְנֶהָ — "bent his bow and strung it" — two completed actions (perfect): the bow is already bent and made ready.
13: לוֹ הֵכִין כְּלֵי-מָוֶת (lô hêkîn kĕlê-māwet, "He has prepared deadly weapons against the persecutors") — hêkîn (hiphil of kûn): to establish, to make firm/ready. The "weapons of death" are prepared specifically for the persecutors. חִצָּיו לַדֹּלְקִים יִפְעַל (ḥiṣṣāyw laddōlĕqîm yipʿal, "his arrows will be fiery shafts") — dālaq: to pursue hotly, to inflame; those who hotly pursue the Psalmist become the targets of flaming arrows. The imagery picks up the ancient Canaanite war-god iconography while applying it to the LORD's justice.
14: הִנֵּה יְחַבֶּל-אָוֶן (hinnēh yĕḥabbel-ʾāwen, "Look: the wicked person conceives evil") — three birth-metaphors for the production of evil: conceiving (yĕḥabbel, piel of ḥābal: to be in birth-pangs, to conceive), becoming pregnant (הָרָה, hārāh: pregnant), giving birth (וְיָלַד, wĕyālad: to bear/give birth). Evil is not accidental but the product of a deliberate generative process — it is what the wicked person produces when they are most fully themselves.
15: בּוֹר כָּרָה וַיַּחְפְּרֵהוּ (bôr kārāh wayyaḥpĕrêhû, "He dug a pit, made it deep") — two verbs for the excavation work: kārāh (to dig) and ḥāpar (to dig/search): he dug and then deepened it. The pit is the trap set for another — but וַיִּפֹּל בְּשַׁחַת יִפְעָל (wayyippōl bĕshaḥat yipʿal): "and fell into the very pit he made." The self-entrapment is the theme of vv15-16 — the retributive principle built into the moral order: evil returns on its maker.
16: יָשׁוּב עֲמָלוֹ בְרֹאשׁוֹ (yāshûb ʿămālô bĕrōʾshô, "His mischief comes back on his own head") — ʿāmāl (trouble, mischief, toil — the laborious effort put into evil); it "returns" (yāshûb) to the head — the top of the one who worked it. עַל-קָדְקֳדוֹ חֲמָסוֹ יֵרֵד (ʿal-qodqodô ḥāmāsô yêrêd, "his violence falls down on his own skull") — ḥāmās (violence) descending on the qodqōd (skull/crown of the head). The image is of the violence thrown upward returning on its thrower.
17: אוֹדֶה יְהוָה כְּצִדְקוֹ (ʾôdeh YHWH kĕṣidqô, "I will give thanks to the LORD for his righteousness") — the psalm closes where it began: God's righteousness. The same ṣedeq appealed to in v8 (vindicate me according to my righteousness) is now the content of praise: God's righteousness is itself the reason for thanksgiving. אֲזַמְּרָה שֵׁם-יְהוָה עֶלְיוֹן (ʾăzammĕrāh shêm-YHWH ʿelyôn) — "sing praise to the name of the LORD Most High" — ʿelyôn (Most High, the Canaanite divine title applied to the LORD) completes the psalm with the highest divine title, appropriate for the cosmic judge of vv7-8.
